name: United States
summary: The highest ceiling and the highest variance: the world's best tech/AI salaries (FAANG $300k+), the #1 AI ecosystem, MIT/Stanford/CMU, English-native, dual citizenship retained (US is on Bangladesh's DNC list), a 3-year STEM-OPT runway, the largest Bangladeshi-American community (~600,000, NYC) — and, crucially, Bangladesh is NOT employment-green-card-backlogged (unlike India). But the access path is brutal and uncertain: the H-1B lottery (~25% odds, plus a new $100,000 fee), Trump-era F-1 social-media vetting and restriction, no universal healthcare, elevated gun-violence/safety concerns and a hostile immigration-politics climate. Derived overall sits below the 60% inclusion line — pulled down by the path, not the prize.

links.title: USCIS — official (immigration & naturalization)
US Department of State — student visas (official)

job-market.summary: The world's deepest tech/AI market with the highest salaries, but hiring is gated by the H-1B lottery and disrupted by 2025–26 layoffs.

pros.text: The world's #1 AI ecosystem and the highest pay anywhere: median SWE ~$133k, FAANG L4 total comp ~$306k, AI/ML specialists $145k–350k+ (LLM seniors $240–350k base).
Many deep hubs (Silicon Valley, NYC, Seattle, Austin) absorb two engineers in an English-native market.

cons.text: Work authorisation for foreign graduates depends on the H-1B lottery (~25% odds) — now with a $100,000 supplemental fee (2025) deterring sponsors.
Q1 2026 saw ~82,000 tech layoffs, adding volatility on top of the visa risk.

links.title: Boundless — FY2026 H-1B cap reached
whatisthesalary — FAANG software engineer total comp (2025–26)

visa-access.summary: A full embassy in Dhaka, but Trump-era F-1 scrutiny — mandatory public social-media vetting, 'hostility' screening and 2025 appointment suspensions — sharply raises refusal risk for Bangladeshi applicants.

pros.text: The US has a full embassy in Dhaka, and Bangladesh is notably NOT on the expanded 2025 travel ban.

cons.text: F-1 applicants must make 5 years of social-media accounts public and are screened for 'hostility', with 2025 appointment suspensions and rising refusals under the Trump administration.
High tuition plus funds proof raise the financial bar, and processing is uncertain.

links.title: The Business Standard — US Embassy Dhaka social-media vetting for student visas
Shorelight — US visa social-media vetting (2026)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship retained both ways and a 5-year-after-green-card path with a native-English civics test — but reaching the green card depends on clearing the H-1B/EB bottleneck first.

pros.text: The US allows dual citizenship with no renunciation, and the US is on Bangladesh's DNC list — so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
Naturalisation is available 5 years after the green card, with English (native) and a civics test (the new 128-question version from Oct 2025).

cons.text: The 5-year clock only starts at the green card — and reaching the green card depends on the H-1B lottery or an EB petition, so the realistic timeline from arrival is long and uncertain.
Immigration policy is highly unstable under the current administration.

links.title: USCIS — naturalization eligibility (official)

post-study-work.summary: STEM graduates get a strong 3-year OPT runway (1 yr + 24-month STEM extension), but the conversion to H-1B is a lottery and OPT itself faces political threat.

pros.text: A STEM master's earns OPT (1 year) plus a 24-month STEM extension = 3 years of work authorisation — a strong runway; each STEM graduate spouse qualifies for their own.

cons.text: Converting OPT to long-term work means the H-1B lottery (~25% + $100k fee), the key bottleneck.
OPT work must relate to the field of study (STEM OPT needs an E-Verify employer), and OPT is under political/legal threat.

links.title: InternationalStudent.com — OPT, STEM OPT & H-1B (2026)

spouse-family.summary: A relative weakness: an F-2 student spouse cannot work, and H-4 work authorisation is restricted to later green-card stages — so the couple effectively needs both on their own F-1/OPT.

pros.text: Children attend public K-12 school free, and if both spouses study (each on F-1) each gets their own OPT/STEM-OPT and H-1B chance.

cons.text: An F-2 dependant spouse cannot work at all, and an H-4 spouse can only work once the H-1B holder reaches an approved I-140 (green-card stage) — and H-4 EAD is under political threat.

links.title: USCIS — H-4 dependants & employment authorization (official)

msc-study.summary: The world's best CS/AI universities (MIT, Stanford, CMU, Berkeley) feeding a 3-year STEM-OPT, but international tuition is extremely high ($50k–80k/yr).

pros.text: MIT, Stanford, CMU and Berkeley are the world's top CS/AI universities; a STEM master's qualifies for the 3-year OPT runway and feeds the strongest job market anywhere.

cons.text: International master's tuition is extremely high ($50k–80k/yr) and funding for taught master's is limited (better at PhD level); student work is on-campus-only during study.

links.title: US Dept of State — study in the USA (official)

pr-pathway.summary: A key structural advantage — Bangladesh is NOT employment-green-card-backlogged (unlike India) — so EB-2/EB-3 (or EB-2 NIW for strong AI candidates) can be fast IF the H-1B/work-status bottleneck is cleared.

pros.text: Crucially, Bangladesh-born applicants are NOT in the multi-decade EB-2/EB-3 backlog that India/China face, so once on the path the green card can be relatively quick; EB-2 NIW lets strong AI researchers self-petition without the lottery.

cons.text: The path still hinges on clearing the H-1B lottery (or a NIW), and a failed lottery or an OPT gap can force departure mid-process.
Policy is highly unpredictable under the current administration.

links.title: Arvian Immigration — F-1/OPT/STEM OPT to EB-2 or EB-3 (2026)

income-cost.summary: The world's highest salaries drive an exceptional surplus, but study-phase tuition/healthcare costs and coastal housing are brutal.

pros.text: Once employed, the world's highest tech salaries (FAANG $300k+) produce an exceptional net surplus and remittance capacity, especially in lower-tax/lower-cost hubs (Austin, Seattle).

cons.text: The study phase is extremely expensive ($50k–80k tuition plus mandatory student health insurance), and Silicon Valley/NYC housing is among the world's costliest.

links.title: whatisthesalary — software engineer salary, US (2025–26)

healthcare.summary: A major weakness: no universal healthcare — coverage is employer-tied or expensive private, students pay $2k–5k/yr mandatory insurance, and out-of-pocket costs and gaps are high.

pros.text: Care quality is high and waits are short for the well-insured (e.g. through a tech employer's plan).

cons.text: There is no universal healthcare: coverage is employer-tied or costly private, students pay mandatory university insurance ($2k–5k/yr), and out-of-pocket costs, deductibles and coverage gaps are high — a serious risk between jobs/visas.
The welfare safety net and (un-subsidised) childcare are weak by peer-country standards.

links.title: USA.gov — health insurance (official)

culture-language.summary: English-native with no language barrier for status and an enormous, diverse South-Asian fabric — though work culture is intense and the political climate is polarised.

pros.text: English is the native language (no language friction for work, study or citizenship), and US cities (especially NYC) have an enormous, diverse South-Asian community that eases adaptation.

cons.text: US work culture is intense (limited statutory leave), and the political climate is polarised with a hostile immigration tone.

links.title: Bangladeshi Americans (overview)

safety-law.summary: Mid-tier rule of law (WJP 27th) but with high gun violence, harsh 2025 immigration enforcement and a polarised climate that dent safety for migrants.

pros.text: Functioning courts and constitutional protections (WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 27th of 143), with strong free-exercise protection for religion.

cons.text: Gun violence and uneven urban crime lower everyday safety, and 2025 immigration enforcement (ICE detentions, due-process concerns) is aggressive toward non-citizens.

links.title: World Justice Project — United States ranks 27th (2025 profile)

politics.summary: The most hostile immigration-politics environment in the set: the Trump administration is restricting H-1B ($100k fee), F-1 scrutiny, OPT and expanding travel bans, in a deeply polarised climate.

pros.text: Despite restriction, the US still issues 85,000 H-1Bs and many EB green cards annually, and elections/courts remain functioning.

cons.text: The administration is actively restricting skilled migration (the $100k H-1B fee, OPT under threat, F-1 social-media vetting) with extreme policy volatility.
Expanding travel bans (19 countries fully banned by Jan 2026) and Muslim-majority-country scrutiny raise targeted risk, even though Bangladesh is not currently banned.

links.title: American Immigration Council — Trump's expanded travel ban

tax.summary: A moderate income-tax burden (federal to 37% + state, lower than most of Europe) with joint-filing benefits and a US–Bangladesh treaty — but no expat regime and healthcare costs sit outside tax.

pros.text: The effective tax burden is moderate (lower than most of Europe), married couples benefit from joint filing, and a US–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: There is no special expat-relief regime, and large healthcare costs effectively sit outside the tax system (paid privately).

links.title: IRS — taxation of nonresident/resident aliens (official)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Among the strongest diaspora fits: the largest Bangladeshi-American community (~600,000, two-thirds in NYC) with hundreds of mosques and abundant halal, tempered by the current anti-Muslim political climate.

pros.text: The US hosts ~600,000 Bangladeshi-Americans — two-thirds in NYC ('Little Bangladesh' in Kensington/Parkchester/Jackson Heights) — within NYC's 750,000+ Muslims and hundreds of mosques, with abundant halal and a vast South-Asian ecosystem.
First-Amendment free-exercise protection for religion is constitutionally strong.

cons.text: The current administration's anti-Muslim/anti-immigrant rhetoric and travel-ban expansions strain day-to-day sentiment, even with Bangladesh not currently banned.

links.title: Bangladeshi Americans (overview)
A Journey through NYC religions — 175+ mosques in NYC

direct-work-route.summary: High ceiling, hard front door. The main work-visa ENTRY (H-1B) is an annual lottery (FY2026 ~35% selection) and the Sept-2025 $100,000 supplemental fee lands squarely on the hire-from-abroad case (new petitions for beneficiaries outside the US) — so direct overseas hiring is the weak point and employers increasingly prefer candidates already on US soil (F-1/OPT → change of status). O-1/L-1 are niche. What rescues the route is the destination, not the entry: Bangladesh is NOT employment-based green-card backlogged (unlike India/China), and the EB-2 NIW lets a strong AI researcher self-petition with no employer, no PERM and no lottery — but that realistically needs the planned MSc (the 2022 BSc + ~4 years is short of the bachelor's+5-years EB-2 equivalence). Largest BD-American community (~600k, NYC). Net: study-first is the realistic US play, not direct work-visa entry.

pros.text: PR ceiling is genuinely high: Bangladesh avoids the India/China EB green-card backlog, and EB-2 NIW allows self-petition with no employer/PERM/lottery (best unlocked by the planned MSc).
Largest Bangladeshi-American community (~600k, two-thirds in NYC); dual citizenship retained both ways (US on Bangladesh's DNC list).

cons.text: Direct entry is lottery-gated (H-1B ~35% selection) and the Sept-2025 $100,000 fee hits exactly the hire-from-Dhaka case — overseas direct hiring is weak.
Sponsorship appetite for overseas juniors is low (cost + lottery risk); Trump-era H-1B/F-1 tightening adds volatility (though Bangladesh is on no travel ban).
